takeaways: Senior housing occupancy hit 89.5% in Q1 — the 19th straight quarterly gain — and is on pace to break 90% this year, while permanent lending sits at multi-year highs ($2.5B in Q4 alone).
The Fed is holding at 3.50%–3.75% with no cut priced before September, yet CRE lending is projected to jump 38% to $805B in 2026 — capital is flowing without rate relief.
The AI adoption gap is stark: 88% of CRE firms are piloting AI but only 5% are hitting program goals — winners embed AI into existing workflows, as Zillow's Gemini partnership shows.

items.headline: Senior housing occupancy climbs to 89.5%, 19th straight quarterly gain
items.summary: NIC MAP data shows occupancy up 230 bps over twelve months, rising ~50 bps per quarter for two-plus years. Industry-wide occupancy should push past 90% later in 2026 — pricing power is shifting to operators like Lloyd Jones as supply stays disciplined.

items.headline: Senior living M&A stays hot: brokers on record deal pace
items.summary: SLIB closed 54 transactions by mid-June, tracking toward its highest volume ever. Stacked Stone Ventures expects $150M in closings within 60–90 days and up to $250M in new deals by year-end. Buyers favor Class A stabilized assets and are moving into secondary markets — competitive tailwind for sellers, tighter field for acquirers.

items.headline: Senior housing financing hits multi-year highs as lenders compete
items.summary: Permanent lending reached ~$4.2B in H2 2025 including $2.5B in Q4 — the strongest since mid-2019. Growing lender competition means better spreads and terms for well-occupied communities; a favorable window for refinancing or acquisition debt.

items.headline: Fed holds at 3.50%–3.75%; no cut priced before September
items.summary: After three 25-bp cuts, the Fed is signaling patience as inflation stays sticky. The 10-year Treasury has stabilized at 4.00%–4.25%, keeping fixed-rate CRE pricing predictable. Underwrite to current rates, not hoped-for cuts.

items.headline: CRE lending projected to surge 38% to $805B in 2026
items.summary: Analysts forecast lending volume jumping from $583B in 2025, with all debt sources active and ample dry powder. Stabilized assets with clean cash flow get the most competitive terms — multifamily and senior housing in supply-constrained markets are prime beneficiaries.

items.headline: Zillow partners with Google Gemini for AI-powered rental search
items.summary: U.S. renters can now discover listings, compare properties, and schedule tours directly through Gemini. AI assistants are becoming a leasing channel — operators should ensure listing data is structured for AI discovery, not just portal search.

items.headline: 88% of CRE firms run AI pilots — only 5% hit their goals
items.summary: JLL's Global Real Estate Technology Survey exposes a massive execution gap between AI experimentation and results. The strongest startups now embed intelligence into existing systems rather than replacing them — a lesson for operators evaluating AI vendors.

items.headline: LTC Properties upsizes credit facility to $1.1B; $41.1M construction loan on Long Island
items.summary: LTC boosted commitments from $800M to $1.1B, and Benchmark Senior Living with National Development secured a JLL-arranged $41.1M construction loan for a new East Islip, NY community. Fresh evidence that both REIT credit and construction debt are open for senior housing.

items.headline: Newmark: stable rates beat Fed cuts for CRE returns
items.summary: Historical analysis shows CRE generated its strongest forward returns during stable-rate periods, not cutting cycles. With the Fed on hold, today's environment may be more favorable for acquisitions than investors waiting for cuts assume.

items.headline: GSEs raise multifamily loan caps 20% to $88B each
items.summary: Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac expanded capacity as MBA projects a 24% rise in total CRE lending. Agency debt remains the backbone of multifamily and seniors housing finance — more room for competitive takeout financing this year.

items.headline: Xpanner raises $18M to retrofit construction equipment with AI
items.summary: The Series B backs an Automation-as-a-Service model that adds robotics and physical AI to existing machinery rather than replacing fleets. Signals where proptech capital is going: augmenting infrastructure the industry already owns.

items.headline: Harmony Senior Services deploys AI cleaning robots across communities
items.summary: The operator rolled out AI-powered cleaning robots to improve efficiency and support staff. A concrete example of AI easing senior living's labor pressure in back-of-house operations — worth benchmarking for portfolio-wide housekeeping costs.

items.headline: JPMorgan: 58% of leaders bet on new products; uncertainty tops concerns
items.summary: The 2026 Business Leaders Outlook shows growth via new products/services up from 53% to 58%, partnerships up to 49%, and M&A appetite at 39% (+8 pts). Economic uncertainty is now the #1 challenge, cited by 49% of leaders, with tariffs emerging at 31%.

items.headline: HBR flags the gap between the company leaders run and employees experience
items.summary: This week's HBR Insider highlights a widening perception gap between executive intent and employee reality. For operators managing dispersed community teams, closing that gap is a retention lever as much as a culture one.

items.headline: Wellness pivots from self-tracking to meaning and emotional repair
items.summary: The Global Wellness Summit's defining 2026 trend is a cultural shift away from measurement and self-surveillance toward meaning, sensation, and emotional connection. Directly relevant to resident-experience programming in senior living.

items.headline: Leadership development tops HR priorities for 2026
items.summary: Leadership development remains HR's #1 priority with innovation enablement rising fast, but many leaders lack capacity to manage change and develop talent simultaneously. Stretched middle management is the bottleneck to watch.

items.headline: Communal wellness surges: social saunas, group fitness, alcohol-free socializing
items.summary: Wellness is becoming social again — group breathwork, community fitness, and connection-driven gatherings are replacing alcohol-centered socializing. A programming cue for senior living communities competing on lifestyle.

items.headline: 'Movement snacks' and AI wearables reshape everyday fitness
items.summary: Short movement bursts throughout the day are displacing long workouts, while wearables pair biometric data with AI-generated fitness plans. Both trends translate well to active-adult and independent-living wellness programs.
